rapSheet.title: Concord: dead in 14 days
rapSheet.detail: Sony's $40 hero shooter was taken offline two weeks after launch. Refunds were issued; developer Firewalk Studios was closed a month later.

rapSheet.title: Tried to delete purchased TV seasons from accounts
rapSheet.detail: Sony announced that Discovery content players had bought on PlayStation Store would be removed from their libraries due to a licensing change. It reversed course only after public outcry.

rapSheet.title: Helldivers 2 PSN mandate
rapSheet.detail: Months after launch, Sony tried to force PC players to link PSN accounts — in 177 countries where PSN accounts can't legally be created, making the game they bought unplayable. Reversed after 200,000+ negative reviews.

rapSheet.title: LittleBigPlanet 3 servers made 'permanently offline'
rapSheet.detail: A 'temporary' outage was quietly made permanent, cutting access to an estimated 10 million player-created levels built over a decade.

rapSheet.title: Tried to close the PS3/Vita stores
rapSheet.detail: Sony announced the closure of PS3 and Vita digital stores — which would have ended access to thousands of digital-only games — then partially reversed after backlash. The PSP store still closed.

rapSheet.title: Anthem switched off completely
rapSheet.detail: The always-online $60 loot shooter became unplayable for everyone — disc owners included. EA shut down at least ten games in 2026 alone.

rapSheet.title: Battlefield Hardline console servers off
rapSheet.detail: Part of EA's rolling yearly purge of older titles. EA maintains a standing 'Service Updates' page because games dying is routine business.

rapSheet.title: Four free-to-play games killed in one day
rapSheet.detail: Need for Speed World, Battlefield Heroes, Battlefield Play4Free and FIFA World were all switched off simultaneously.

rapSheet.title: Darkspore: single-player killed by DRM servers
rapSheet.detail: A paid single-player game with always-online DRM became permanently unplayable when its authentication servers went dark — a decade-early warning of everything that followed.

quote.text: Gamers need to get comfortable with not owning their games.
quote.attribution: Philippe Tremblay, Ubisoft director of subscriptions (paraphrased from a 2024 interview)

rapSheet.title: The Crew: killed and revoked
rapSheet.detail: After nine years, Ubisoft shut The Crew's servers and revoked the licenses from buyers' libraries. In the resulting California lawsuit, Ubisoft's defense argued players had never owned the game — only bought a limited license.

rapSheet.title: XDefiant dead within a year
rapSheet.detail: Ubisoft's free-to-play Call of Duty rival was sunset less than seven months after launch and fully off within about a year.

rapSheet.title: Online services decommissioned for 90+ older games
rapSheet.detail: A sweeping decommissioning wave ended online play — and in some cases access to purchased DLC — across dozens of legacy Ubisoft titles.

rapSheet.title: Offline modes pledged for The Crew 2 and Motorfest
rapSheet.detail: After the backlash and lawsuit over The Crew, Ubisoft committed to adding offline modes to its sequels — proof that pressure works.

rapSheet.title: MultiVersus shut down (twice)
rapSheet.detail: The platform fighter went dark once as a 'beta', relaunched, then shut down for good a year later. An offline mode preserving owned fighters was its parting gift.

rapSheet.title: Suicide Squad: offline mode shipped at the end
rapSheet.detail: After launching always-online, Kill the Justice League got an offline mode in its final update — the right ending to the wrong design.

rapSheet.title: Babylon's Fall: $60, dead in under a year
rapSheet.detail: The PlatinumGames live-service action game was announced for shutdown five months after release and switched off before its first birthday.

rapSheet.title: Marvel's Avengers delisted
rapSheet.detail: Support ended and the game was pulled from sale three years after a full-priced live-service launch.

rapSheet.title: Paragon closed — with full refunds
rapSheet.detail: Epic killed its MOBA to focus on Fortnite, refunded every purchase, and released $17M of its assets to developers for free. Cited here as the standard others fail to meet.

rapSheet.title: Rumbleverse dead in six months
rapSheet.detail: Purchases were refunded when the brawler-royale shut down 201 days after launch.

rapSheet.title: Classic Unreal and Unreal Tournament delisted, master servers off
rapSheet.detail: Epic removed the genre-defining classics from sale and shut their master servers; community workarounds keep parts alive.

rapSheet.title: Wii U and 3DS online services terminated
rapSheet.detail: Online play, matchmaking and cooperative features ended for two entire console generations at once, affecting hundreds of games.

rapSheet.title: Wii U / 3DS eShops closed
rapSheet.detail: Over a thousand digital-only games became impossible to buy. Nintendo committed to re-download access only 'for the foreseeable future'.

rapSheet.title: Switch 2 'game-key cards'
rapSheet.detail: Many Switch 2 boxed releases contain no game data — the card is a download key in a box. Physical preservation, hollowed out.

rapSheet.title: Pocket Camp: the soft landing
rapSheet.detail: When Animal Crossing: Pocket Camp's servers closed, Nintendo shipped a paid offline version that carries your data over — proof a graceful end is possible when a publisher cares.

rapSheet.title: Forza Horizon 4 delisted
rapSheet.detail: The fourth Horizon game in a row removed from sale as music and car licenses expired — a metronome of disappearing racers.

rapSheet.title: Xbox 360 Store closed
rapSheet.detail: The digital storefront for an entire console generation shut down; previously purchased content remains re-downloadable, new purchases are gone forever.

rapSheet.title: Redfall's final update added offline play
rapSheet.detail: After closing developer Arkane Austin, Microsoft at least shipped an update making Redfall playable offline forever. The bar is on the floor; this cleared it.

rapSheet.title: Overwatch 1: overwritten, not shut down
rapSheet.detail: The $40 game roughly 50 million people bought was replaced in place by a free-to-play sequel. There is no way to play the product that was sold.

rapSheet.title: Warzone Mobile sunset
rapSheet.detail: The mobile battle royale was switched off barely two years after launch.

rapSheet.title: Warcraft III: Reforged replaced the classic client
rapSheet.detail: The remaster overwrote the original game's client and removed features it had shipped with for 18 years, breaking promises made at announcement.

rapSheet.title: Spellbreak shut down after Proletariat acqui-hire
rapSheet.detail: Blizzard bought the studio to work on World of Warcraft; the battle-royale it was running was closed.

rapSheet.title: WWE 2K24 servers off after ~22 months
rapSheet.detail: 2K's standing pattern: annual sports titles lose their online modes — and server-dependent single-player modes like MyCareer — roughly two years after you pay $70.

rapSheet.title: Battleborn erased
rapSheet.detail: The $60 hero shooter became fully unplayable, campaign included.

rapSheet.title: GTA Online closed on PS3/Xbox 360
rapSheet.detail: The era's biggest online game ended for the consoles it launched on.

rapSheet.title: PUBG: Blindspot dead in 8 weeks
rapSheet.detail: The early-access spin-off launched February 4, 2026 and was gone by March 30.

rapSheet.title: Crucible un-launched, then killed
rapSheet.detail: Amazon's debut shooter went back to closed beta within two months of launch and was cancelled within six.

rapSheet.title: King of Meat shut down within a year
rapSheet.detail: The co-op dungeon brawler was switched off months after release.

rapSheet.title: Echo VR switched off
rapSheet.detail: VR's defining social sport was shut down despite tens of thousands of signatures and an offer from the community to run it themselves.

rapSheet.title: LawBreakers dead in 13 months
rapSheet.detail: The $30 arena shooter shut down with no offline option.

rapSheet.title: Original KartRider killed after 19 years
rapSheet.detail: The long-running racer was closed to push players to its successor.

rapSheet.title: City of Heroes closed while profitable
rapSheet.detail: The beloved superhero MMO was shut down abruptly. Twelve years later NCSoft finally licensed the community's resurrected servers.

rapSheet.title: WildStar erased
rapSheet.detail: Every character, world and purchase gone.

rapSheet.title: Club Penguin closed
rapSheet.detail: The kids' MMO with hundreds of millions of registered accounts was shut to push a mobile replacement — which itself died the following year.

rapSheet.title: Disney Infinity cancelled
rapSheet.detail: Disney exited games publishing, orphaning a toys-to-life platform families had spent an estimated $1.5 billion on. PC and mobile versions were switched off entirely.

rapSheet.title: Genshin Impact ended PS4 support
rapSheet.detail: The PS4 version was sunset; account progression carries forward only on newer hardware.

rapSheet.title: CS:GO replaced in place by Counter-Strike 2
rapSheet.detail: The game 25 million people played was overwritten overnight. A limited legacy branch exists without official matchmaking.

rapSheet.title: Steam Subscriber Agreement: you buy licenses, not games
rapSheet.detail: Steam's own terms say purchases are 'subscriptions' with no ownership right. In 2024, California law forced storefronts to start admitting this at checkout.

rapSheet.title: Highguard dead in six weeks
rapSheet.detail: 2 million players at launch in late January 2026; funding pulled; servers off March 12. One of the shortest-lived major releases ever.

rapSheet.title: Destiny 2 'Content Vault' removed paid campaigns
rapSheet.detail: Entire expansions players paid for — including the base campaign — were deleted from the live game. The purchases were not refunded.

rapSheet.title: The Day Before: 46 days
rapSheet.detail: Steam's most-wishlisted game shut down 46 days after launch; the studio announced its own closure four days in. Steam refunded broadly.

rapSheet.title: Gundam Evolution dead in 14 months
rapSheet.detail: The free-to-play hero shooter was switched off just over a year after launch.

rapSheet.title: P.T. erased from existence
rapSheet.detail: The 'Playable Teaser' was delisted and made un-redownloadable. Consoles with it installed became collector's items.

rapSheet.title: PES replaced by free-to-play eFootball
rapSheet.detail: A 26-year-old paid franchise ended; its live-service replacement launched broken and wiped the old games' relevance overnight.

rapSheet.title: Star Wars Galaxies closed
rapSheet.detail: Shut down ahead of The Old Republic's launch; a generation's characters and cities deleted.

rapSheet.title: CrossfireX shut down — paid campaign included
rapSheet.detail: The Remedy-developed single-player episodes people bought became permanently unplayable 15 months after launch.

rapSheet.title: Defiance and Defiance 2050 killed the same day
rapSheet.detail: Both versions of the sci-fi shooter MMO went dark together.

rapSheet.title: Gigantic dead after a year
rapSheet.detail: Later resurrected by different hands in 2024 as a paid, offline-capable re-release — dead games can come back when someone cares.

rapSheet.title: DRM retrofitted onto old games
rapSheet.detail: Capcom added new DRM to years-old PC titles, breaking mods and, briefly, the games themselves — a reminder that a storefront copy can change under you.

rapSheet.title: Wayfinder went from online-only to fully offline
rapSheet.detail: After its publisher walked away, the developer bought the game back and rebuilt it as an offline single-player title instead of shutting it down. It can be done.
